title: Reading and Use of English - Part 7
description: You are going to read an article about four people who are professional or serious photographers and talk about their work. For questions 43–52, choose from the people (A–D). The people may be chosen more than once.

content.title: Through the Lens: Four Photographers on Their Work

texts.A:
### Imelda, 36, Documentary Photographer
I've been documenting communities facing displacement and poverty for twelve years, working primarily in East Africa and South-East Asia. [43]My work is driven by a belief that photography can make visible what institutions prefer to ignore – the faces behind statistics, the individuals behind categories.[/43] I shoot with a small film camera rather than digital equipment, which forces a slower, more deliberate and more respectful engagement with my subjects. [47]The hardest aspect of my work is managing the ethical weight of photographing people in crisis – the responsibility to represent suffering accurately rather than sensationally keeps me awake at night far more than any technical challenge.[/47] [51]I measure success not in awards but in outcomes – one of my photo essays directly contributed to a UN-funded relief programme being established, which remains the most meaningful thing I've done professionally.[/51]
texts.B:
### Victor, 48, Wedding Photographer
[44]I fell into wedding photography almost by accident, shooting a friend's wedding as a favour fifteen years ago and discovering I had a genuine talent for capturing unposed, emotional moments.[/44] I now shoot around forty weddings a year, which sounds glamorous but involves an enormous amount of invisible labour – consultations, equipment preparation and hours of post-processing for every hour spent actually shooting. [48]What people don't realise is the psychological pressure of a wedding – there are no second chances, every moment is fleeting, and the consequences of missing a key shot are significant and very personal to the couple.[/48] [52]I've turned down more lucrative commercial work to stay focused on weddings because I love the human story at the centre of each one and couldn't give up that emotional connection for a better-paying product shoot.[/52]
texts.C:
### Asha, 27, Wildlife Photographer
[45]I spend up to six months a year in the field, often in remote locations with unreliable power, extreme temperatures and unpredictable wildlife, and I consider it the greatest privilege imaginable.[/45] I came to wildlife photography through conservation biology – my images are tools for telling stories about species under threat. [49]Patience is a professional requirement in this work in a way it simply isn't in any other photography genre – I once spent three days in a hide waiting for a snow leopard to emerge, and when it finally did, the shot itself lasted four seconds.[/49] I publish my work with conservation organisations and donate all proceeds from print sales to habitat protection projects.
texts.D:
### Phil, 60, Street Photographer
[46]I've been photographing city streets since I was a teenager with a secondhand camera, and what drew me then still draws me now – the unscripted, unposed reality of public life.[/46] Street photography requires a particular kind of invisibility; you have to move through a space in a way that doesn't alter what's happening around you, which takes years to master. [50]I've published four books of street photographs, and the one I'm most proud of documents twenty years of change in my own neighbourhood, functioning almost as a social history of that place.[/50] Critics sometimes question whether photographing strangers in public is ethical, and I think about this constantly – it's a tension that keeps the practice honest.

questions.question: describes their work as a means of drawing attention to people or situations that are being overlooked?
questions.explanation: Imelda says her work is driven by a belief that photography can make visible what institutions prefer to ignore.

questions.question: moved into their area of photography without having specifically planned to do so?
questions.explanation: Victor says he fell into wedding photography almost by accident, originally shooting a friend's wedding as a favour.

questions.question: describes working in physically demanding or remote conditions as an integral part of the job they love?
questions.explanation: Asha spends up to six months a year in remote locations with unreliable power and extreme temperatures and calls it the greatest privilege.

questions.question: has been working in the same style of photography since they were very young?
questions.explanation: Phil says he has been photographing city streets since he was a teenager with a secondhand camera.

questions.question: says that the ethical responsibilities of their work are a greater challenge than the technical aspects?
questions.explanation: Imelda says the ethical weight of photographing people in crisis keeps her awake far more than any technical challenge.

questions.question: highlights the pressure of working in situations where errors or missed opportunities cannot be corrected?
questions.explanation: Victor says there are no second chances at a wedding, every moment is fleeting, and missing a key shot has significant personal consequences.

questions.question: provides a specific example of extreme patience required in their photographic work?
questions.explanation: Asha once spent three days in a hide waiting for a snow leopard to emerge, and the resulting shot lasted just four seconds.

questions.question: has produced a body of work that records changes in a specific place over a long period of time?
questions.explanation: Phil's most-prized book documents twenty years of change in his own neighbourhood, functioning almost as a social history.

questions.question: judges the value of their work by its real-world consequences rather than by critical or commercial recognition?
questions.explanation: Imelda says she measures success in outcomes rather than awards – a photo essay contributing to a UN relief programme is the most meaningful thing she's done.

questions.question: deliberately chose to remain in an emotionally rewarding area of their profession over a more financially attractive one?
questions.explanation: Victor has turned down more lucrative commercial work to stay focused on weddings because of the emotional connection the human story provides.
